Query: Helmet cells are characteristic of anemia of

Document [0] (Title: Acute Anemia -- Histopathology) When examining a peripheral blood smear under a microscope, specific findings can provide valuable insights into the underlying condition. Observations related to certain types of anemia are as follows: Microangiopathic hemolysis : In conditions characterized by microangiopathic hemolysis, such as TTP, HUS, and DIC, a peripheral blood smear examination may reveal several abnormal RBC shapes. These abnormalities include helmet cells, fragmented RBCs (schistocytes), and other RBC fragments. Autoimmune hemolytic anemias or hereditary spherocytosis : Spherocytes (small, round RBCs lacking central pallor) may be present in some cases.
Document [1] (Title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Histopathology) RBC shape is crucial in diagnosis. A distinctive blood smear may even be sufficient for the diagnosis of a type of hemolytic anemia. This can be seen in cases such as hereditary elliptocytosis or Southeast Asian ovalocytosis.
Document [2] (Title: Poikilocytosis -- Pearls and Other Issues) The most common types of poikilocytosis are sickle cells, target cells, spherocytes, elliptocytes, ovalocytes, echinocytes, and acanthocytes.
Document [3] (Title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Differential Diagnosis) Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) is a common cause of hemolysis, and it commonly presents in adult patients. Unlike elliptocytosis, however, patients with AIHA commonly test positive for Coombs, and this disease does not commonly correlate with a positive family history. [15] [16]
Document [4] (Title: Poikilocytosis -- Evaluation) Not all RBCs will take on an abnormal shape. Patients with poikilocytosis have some normally shaped cells RBCs mixed with abnormally shaped poikilocytes. Sometimes, many different types of poikilocytes are present in one patient's blood smear in conditions like iron deficiency anemia, megaloblastic anemia, etc.
Document [5] (Title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Etiology) Membranopathies include hereditary spherocytosis (HS) and hereditary elliptocytosis (HE). HS is often autosomal dominant; however, non-dominant and recessive traits have been seen. It has been seen in all racial groups. HS has been documented as a rare disease, however, due to limited knowledge as the onset and severity vary considerably, as well as, the lack of specific lab tests, make it a difficult disease to study. HE is a heterogeneous red cell membrane disordered where the autosomal dominant inheritance can lead to a spectrum of presentations from asymptomatic to life-threatening. [4] [5]
Document [6] (Title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Clinical Significance) Erythrocyte size, distribution, and shape variation can all be clues to clinical disease and pathologic processes. For example, acanthocytes may be a byproduct of eryptosis or organized erythrocyte breakdown and death. This process of tabulated cell destruction has been seen in anemia and excess calcium. Agglutination of red blood cells can be an indication of a hypercoagulable state, and microcytosis or small red blood cells can be associated with different forms of microcytic anemia, such as iron deficiency and thalassemia. [8] Additionally, red blood cell morphology has a significant role in the severity of the disease. For example, in sickle cell disease, the concentration of inherited hemoglobin S determines the degree of erythrocyte sickling.

Document [8] (Title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Pathophysiology) When a red blood cell is unable to change shape as it passes through the spleen, it will become sequestered and phagocytosis will occur. This is seen in hemoglobinopathies such as sickle cell disease.
Document [9] (Title: Poikilocytosis -- Etiology) Sickle cells (drepanocytes): Seen in sickle cell disease.
Document [10] (Title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- Evaluation) Peripheral blood smear and urine analysis are essential for distinguishing between intravascular and extravascular hemolysis in normocytic anemia. The presence of schistocytes and hemoglobin in the urine suggests microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. However, despite suspected hemolysis, the absence of schistocytes in the peripheral blood smear and the absence of hemoglobin in the urine warrant further investigation. Detection of spherocytes in the peripheral blood smear prompts a direct Coombs test to differentiate autoimmune hemolytic anemia from splenic sequestration in hereditary spherocytosis, aiding diagnosis.
Document [11] (Title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- Evaluation) The evaluation of hereditary elliptocytosis is initiated with a comprehensive assessment, including a complete blood count (CBC) revealing normocytic normochromic anemia. A peripheral blood smear is essential, typically demonstrating elliptocytes in 15% to 100% of RBCs, along with other variations, such as spherocytes, stomatocytes, poikilocytes, ovalocytes, and macro-ovalocytes. Testing for hemolysis reveals features of extravascular hemolysis characterized by an elevated reticulocyte count, increased lactate dehydrogenase, indirect bilirubin, and decreased haptoglobin level. The predominance of elliptocytes does not consistently correlate with the severity of hemolysis. Polychromasia is expected with hemolysis and reticulocytosis.
Document [12] (Title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Histopathology) A peripheral blood smear should be studied when there is concern for hemolysis. One would look for abnormal red blood cells such as schistocytes, spherocytes, or bite cells. [10]
Document [13] (Title: Poikilocytosis -- Pearls and Other Issues) The most common etiologies of poikilocytosis are sickle cell disease, thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis, iron deficiency anemia, megaloblastic anemia, and liver disease.
Document [14] (Title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Prognosis) The course of aHUS has traditionally been much less benign than typical HUS; however, the advent of eculizumab treatment has decreased progression to ESRD or death in children from 30% to 50% down to 9% and in adults from 60% to 6% down to 15%. [5]
Document [15] (Title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- Introduction) Hereditary elliptocytosis encompasses various subtypes, each with distinct characteristics. These subtypes include common hereditary elliptocytosis, hereditary pyropoikilocytosis (HPP), Southeast Asian ovalocytosis (SAO), and spherocytic elliptocytosis (SE). The differentiation among these subtypes lies in variations in RBC morphology and the degree of hemolysis.
Document [16] (Title: Acute Anemia -- Etiology -- Hemolytic Anemias) RBC membrane-cytoskeletal defects : Hereditary spherocytosis and elliptocytosis are some conditions that involve these types of RBC abnormalities, resulting in the disfigurement of RBCs, leading to the removal of the spleen. [6] [7] [8] Hereditary spherocytosis causes hemolytic anemia, jaundice, splenomegaly, gallstones, and spherocytes on a peripheral smear with increased osmotic fragility. Hereditary elliptocytosis causes mild to severe hemolytic anemia, jaundice, splenomegaly, and elliptical (oval-shaped) RBCs on a peripheral smear, with most cases being asymptomatic. Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) is an acquired RBC membrane defect caused by a mutation in the PIGA gene in hematopoietic stem cells, leading to the absence of cell surface markers CD55 and CD59. This clonal defect leads to intravascular hemolysis, dark-colored urine (especially in the morning), anemia, fatigue, abdominal pain, thrombosis, and recurrent infections.
Document [17] (Title: Acanthocytosis -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical conditions associated with acanthocytosis:) Severe liver dysfunction: Spur cell anemia (SCA) is a poorly studied clinical entity in liver disease, and its clinical importance remains as yet undefined. It gets diagnosed with the following: Hb is less than10 mg/dl, hemolysis, over 5% spur cells on PBS, and the exclusion of other causes of anemia. Seen in severe liver disease patients regardless of the etiology, it is known to portend an unfavorable prognosis as a majority of patients die within months of diagnosis. SCA is characteristically not amenable to treatment with blood transfusion, as transfused cells tend to undergo morphological alterations as well [2] . It is important to note that SCA is readily reversible after liver transplantation (LT). [6] Although with limited success, there have been reports that have suggested various other modalities of treatment for SCA, such as plasmapheresis, transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt, etc. These treatments may be useful in patients awaiting LT or in those who are poor candidates for LT. [7] [8]
Document [18] (Title: Rh Hemolytic Disease(Archived) -- Histopathology) In Rh-hemolytic disease of the newborn, the following changes are seen in the peripheral blood smear: Polychromasia Anisocytosis Erythroblasts No spherocytes
Document [19] (Title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Introduction) Hereditary spherocytosis (HS) is the most prevalent cause of hemolytic anemia due to an abnormal red cell membrane and classifies as a type of congenital hemolytic anemia. Oskar Minkowsky first described it in the early 1900s. [1] Erythrocytes are unable to maintain their normal biconcave shape due to genetic mutations in membrane/cytoskeletal proteins that play a role in structural morphologic stability. Deficient or abnormal proteins may include spectrin, ankyrin, band 3, and band 4.2, encoded by different genes. [2] [3] The clinical manifestations vary based on the severity of disease and the type of genetic mutation. [1] [2] [3]
Document [20] (Title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Histopathology) In microscopical blood smear evaluation, spherocytic red blood cells appear as smaller round cells lacking the normal central pale area due to the absence of a normal biconcave shape. Spherocytes may also be present in other types of hemolytic anemia, although more commonly in hereditary spherocytosis. [2] [6] Howell-Jolly bodies are frequently present in post-splenectomy blood smear microscopic evaluation; their absence after splenectomy may be a sign of residual functioning splenic tissue. [7]
Document [21] (Title: Poikilocytosis -- Pathophysiology) Schistocytes are fragmented RBCs seen most commonly in hemolytic anemias, hemolytic disorders, DIC, pre-eclampsia, B12 deficiency, HIV, BMT, mechanical heart valves, and COVID infection, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura and hemolytic uremic syndrome. [12] [13] [14]

Document [23] (Title: Shigellosis -- Prognosis) Complications of shigellosis include hemolytic uremic syndrome, defined as a triad of thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, and acute kidney injury. Hemolytic uremic syndrome is a microangiopathic hemolytic anemia; its hallmark is schistocytes seen on a peripheral smear. The mortality rate is up to 50%. [51]

Document [25] (Title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- History and Physical) Common hereditary elliptocytosis represents the most prevalent form of hereditary elliptocytosis, and patients are typically asymptomatic. Neonates with this condition may experience transient hemolysis, which typically resolves within the first year of life. In cases of severe hemolytic anemia and jaundice in neonates, transfusion and phototherapy may be necessary. The defining characteristic of common hereditary elliptocytosis is the presence of elliptical-shaped RBCs in the peripheral blood smear, constituting anywhere from 15% to 100% of the total RBCs. The peripheral blood smear may also observe spherocytes, stomatocytes, and poikilocytes (fragmented cells).
Document [26] (Title: Hereditary Spherocytosis  -- Evaluation) Hereditary spherocytosis has a broad severity spectrum, and patients will have different clinical courses. The degree of anemia categorizes as severe for patients with hemoglobin lower than 8 g/dL, moderate for 8 to 10 g/dL, or mild with hemoglobin 10 to 11.5 g/dL in females, or 10 to 13.5 g/dL in males. [8]

The category Other deficiency anemias encompasses several distinct subtypes, including folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات) and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). It also includes acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) as a child category, as well as non‑immune hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this heterogeneous group helps clinicians consider underlying nutritional deficiencies and hemolytic mechanisms when evaluating and managing affected patients.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538) Other deficiency anemias is a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific types of anemia. It is classified as فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia) and as فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), reflecting its inclusion of nutrient‑deficiency–related blood disorders. In addition, the category is classified as فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune‑mediated hemolytic anemia), indicating that it also encompasses hemolytic anemias that are not caused by autoimmunity. Within this grouping, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia) is identified as a child or subcategory, further specifying a type of hemolysis that develops secondary to external factors. Together, these relationships show that “Other deficiency anemias” serves as an umbrella term that integrates both deficiency‑based anemias (folate and iron) and certain non‑immune hemolytic anemias, including acquired forms, which is important for accurate diagnosis, classification, and patient management.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 47) Anemia associated with chronic kidney disease is placed within the “Other deficiency anemias” group, which also comprises folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), vitamin B12‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Within this category are several sub‑entities: non‑megaloblastic (aplastic) anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), and non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Although thalassemia (الثلاسيميا) is a hemoglobinopathy rather than a classic deficiency anemia, it is organized under the same “Other deficiency anemias” heading, and sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) has been linked to thalassemia, indicating a retinal vascular complication of this hemoglobin disorder. Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239) The collection “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” comprises a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also members of this autoimmune group, illustrating the systemic nature of immune‑mediated disease. Although non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية) is listed among these entities, it does not result from autoantibody‑mediated red‑cell destruction; its inclusion reflects clinical overlap and underscores the importance of distinguishing immune from non‑immune mechanisms when evaluating hemolysis. Recognizing that these diverse conditions are linked by an underlying autoimmune process helps clinicians diagnose, monitor, and manage patients with immune‑driven pathology more effectively.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888) Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with anemia (فقر الدم), as the sudden loss of renal function often leads to reduced production of erythropoietin and impaired clearance of uremic toxins, which together contribute to a decrease in red blood cell count. This relationship highlights the importance of monitoring hemoglobin levels in patients with acute renal injury and addressing anemia promptly to improve oxygen delivery, reduce cardiovascular strain, and support overall recovery. Effective management of both conditions is essential for optimizing patient outcomes in the setting of acute kidney dysfunction.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 433) Anemia in chronic kidney disease is categorized as an “Other deficiency anemia.” The “Other deficiency anemias” classification includes several specific disorders: folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), megaloblastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات), and thalassemia (الثلاسيميا). Thalassemia is clinically associated with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية), indicating that patients with thalassemia may develop retinal complications characteristic of sickle‑cell disease and highlighting the need for appropriate ophthalmologic monitoring.
